title: Distributed Credential Testing: High Card Velocity with Normalized Merchant Patterns
narrative: Compromised card credentials are being systematically tested across multiple merchant categories in a coordinated attack, but each individual merchant sees normal transaction velocity to avoid per-MID detection. The fraud surface manifests in elevated card-level counters (C1, C8, C13) showing the card is touching far more merchants than the cardholder's historical baseline, while per-merchant metrics remain unremarkable. The device and identity context change frequently (high card1_distinct_addr1, elevated D1 first-time pairings), yet individual transaction amounts and timing appear legitimate. Fraudsters exploit the fact that most merchant fraud systems only see their own slice of activity, while card-issuer aggregation reveals the true cross-merchant velocity. This pattern is characteristic of organized credential-testing operations that distribute stolen card data across multiple merchant accounts to stay below individual fraud thresholds while maximizing successful authorizations before the card is blocked.
indicators: C1 > 60 (p90), card used across many transactions in rolling window
C13 > 24 (p90), card appearing at elevated number of distinct merchant categories
C8 > 29 (p90), high card-level counter indicating diverse transaction contexts
card1_distinct_addr1 > 8, card associated with multiple different billing addresses
C14 between 2 and 7 (p75-p90), moderate per-merchant transaction count staying under alerts
D1 = 0 or D-field counters < 3, indicating fresh device-card pairings across sessions
TransactionAmt between 70 and 180 (p25-p75), amounts intentionally kept in normal range
indicator_explanations: Card shows unusually high activity across the rolling time window beyond normal usage
Card is being used across far more merchant categories than typical cardholder behavior
Card-level aggregation reveals activity patterns inconsistent with single legitimate user
Same card number appears with many different billing addresses in short timeframe
Activity at each individual merchant stays below typical fraud velocity thresholds
Card keeps appearing on devices that have never seen it before
Transaction amounts deliberately kept within normal spending range to avoid amount-based flags
summary: Stolen credentials are distributed across multiple merchants with each seeing acceptable transaction counts, but card-level aggregation reveals velocity and diversity far exceeding normal cardholder patterns. Device and address associations constantly change while per-transaction signals remain clean.
reasoning: Legitimate cardholders don't suddenly use their card across dozens of different merchant categories with constantly changing devices and billing addresses while maintaining per-merchant activity below fraud thresholds. This coordinated distribution pattern is diagnostic of organized credential-compromise operations exploiting siloed merchant fraud detection.
